seo_title: Achieving Synergistic Tool Gains for Medical Agents
meta_description: A new approach to medical tool use aims to correct failure instances and improve reliability in clinical settings.
body_html:
Direct answer: Medical AI agents increasingly use external tools for diagnosis, treatment recommendation, and evidence retrieval, yet most existing approaches assume that task-appropriate tools are reliable within their intended scope.

The assumption of tool reliability is fragile in real clinical settings, where even relevant tools may fail on challenging instances and lead to unsafe downstream decisions. Conventional task-level tool selection cannot realize the gap between the best fixed single tool and an ideal instance-wise selector.

Proposed Solution: GRPO-based Reinforcement Learning Framework Our method promotes instance-level correction of erroneous tool consensus through probabilistic risk minimization and disagreement-aware synergy learning.

Key Components of the Proposed Framework

Entropy-guided sampling strategy to upweight high-disagreement instances, providing stronger signals for learning instance-specific tool synergy. GRPO-based reinforcement learning framework with rewards for probabilistic risk minimization and synergy-aware correction of erroneous tool consensus.

Task Baseline Performance Our Method Performance

Experiments on two tasks and seven medical benchmarks show that our method consistently achieves robust and stable improvements over a broad range of baselines, highlighting the importance of synergy-aware tool use for reliable medical agentic systems.

source.title: Mind the Tool Failures: Achieving Synergistic Tool Gains for Medical Agents
source.summary: Researchers have identified a problem with using external tools in medical AI agents, where even relevant tools can fail on challenging instances, leading to unsafe decisions. To address this issue, they propose a new framework that uses reinforcement learning and entropy-guided sampling to promote instance-level correction of erroneous tool consensus and improve tool synergy. The approach aims to mitigate the gap between the best single tool and an ideal instance-wise selector. Experimental results show consistent improvements over baselines on two tasks and seven medical benchmarks.
